flashcards.q: State the universal law of gravitation in one sentence.
flashcards.a: Every object in the universe attracts every other object with a force proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

flashcards.q: Write the formula for gravitational force between two objects.
flashcards.a: F = G × (M × m) / d², where G is the universal gravitational constant, M and m are the masses, and d is the distance between their centres.

flashcards.q: What is the value and SI unit of the universal gravitational constant G?
flashcards.a: G = 6.673 × 10⁻¹¹ N m² kg⁻².

flashcards.q: What does 'universal' mean in the context of the law of gravitation?
flashcards.a: The law applies to all bodies everywhere in the universe — big or small, celestial or terrestrial — and G has the same value throughout.

flashcards.q: If the distance between two objects is doubled, by what factor does the gravitational force change?
flashcards.a: The force becomes 1/4th of the original (since F ∝ 1/d²).

flashcards.q: If the distance between two objects is halved, what happens to the gravitational force?
flashcards.a: The force becomes 4 times the original.

flashcards.q: Name any two phenomena explained by the universal law of gravitation.
flashcards.a: The motion of the moon around the earth, and the motion of planets around the Sun (also: ocean tides, our weight on earth).

flashcards.q: What is centripetal force?
flashcards.a: The force directed towards the centre of a circular path that keeps an object moving in that path. For the moon, it is provided by the earth's gravitational attraction.

flashcards.q: What happens to a stone when the thread holding it in circular motion is released?
flashcards.a: It flies off in a straight line along the tangent to the circular path.

flashcards.q: Define free fall.
flashcards.a: The motion of an object falling towards the earth under the influence of gravitational force alone (no air resistance or other forces).

flashcards.q: What is acceleration due to gravity (g)? What is its SI unit?
flashcards.a: The acceleration produced in a freely falling object due to earth's gravitational force. SI unit: m s⁻². Near earth's surface, g ≈ 9.8 m s⁻².

flashcards.q: Write the formula used to calculate g at the earth's surface.
flashcards.a: g = G × M / R², where M = mass of the earth and R = radius of the earth.

flashcards.q: Does the acceleration due to gravity depend on the mass of the falling object?
flashcards.a: No. g = GM/R² is independent of the mass of the object — all objects fall with the same acceleration in vacuum.

flashcards.q: At which location on earth is g greater — poles or equator? Why?
flashcards.a: At the poles, because the earth's radius is smaller there, and g = GM/R² increases as R decreases.

flashcards.q: Write the three equations of motion applicable to freely falling objects.
flashcards.a: v = u + gt;  s = ut + ½gt²;  v² = u² + 2gs  (with g positive for downward motion, negative for upward).

flashcards.q: An object is thrown upward. What is the sign of g?
flashcards.a: Negative (−g), since gravity opposes the upward motion.

flashcards.q: What is mass? Does it change from place to place?
flashcards.a: Mass is the measure of the inertia (quantity of matter) of an object. It is constant everywhere — on earth, the moon, or in space.

flashcards.q: What is weight? Write its formula and SI unit.
flashcards.a: Weight is the force with which the earth attracts an object. W = m × g. SI unit: newton (N).

flashcards.q: Is weight a scalar or a vector? In which direction does it act?
flashcards.a: Weight is a vector. It acts vertically downward.

flashcards.q: Why does weight vary from place to place while mass does not?
flashcards.a: Weight = mg; since g varies with location, weight changes. Mass depends only on the amount of matter and is always constant.

flashcards.q: What instrument measures mass? What instrument measures weight?
flashcards.a: Mass: beam balance. Weight: spring balance.

flashcards.q: What is the weight of an object on the moon compared to its weight on earth?
flashcards.a: Weight on moon = (1/6) × weight on earth, because the moon's mass and radius give it a weaker gravitational pull.

flashcards.q: Define thrust.
flashcards.a: The force acting on an object perpendicular to its surface. SI unit: newton (N).

flashcards.q: Define pressure and write its formula.
flashcards.a: Pressure is the force (thrust) acting per unit area. P = F / A. SI unit: pascal (Pa) = N m⁻².

flashcards.q: What is 1 pascal (Pa)?
flashcards.a: 1 Pa = 1 N m⁻². It is the SI unit of pressure, named after scientist Blaise Pascal.

flashcards.q: For the same thrust, how does pressure change if the area is halved?
flashcards.a: Pressure doubles (P = F/A; smaller A → larger P).

flashcards.q: Why do cutting tools have sharp edges?
flashcards.a: A sharp edge has a very small contact area, so the same force (thrust) produces a very high pressure, making cutting easier.

flashcards.q: What are fluids? Give two examples.
flashcards.a: Substances that can flow are called fluids. Examples: water (liquid) and air (gas).

flashcards.q: What is buoyancy?
flashcards.a: The tendency of a fluid to exert an upward force (buoyant force / upthrust) on an object immersed in it.

flashcards.q: In which direction does the buoyant force act?
flashcards.a: Vertically upward.

flashcards.q: On what two factors does the magnitude of the buoyant force depend?
flashcards.a: The volume of the object submerged (volume of fluid displaced) and the density of the fluid.

flashcards.q: State Archimedes' Principle.
flashcards.a: When a body is immersed fully or partially in a fluid, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by it.

flashcards.q: Once an object is fully submerged, does the buoyant force increase if pushed deeper?
flashcards.a: No. Once fully immersed, no more fluid is displaced, so the buoyant force remains constant regardless of depth.

flashcards.q: Define density. Write its formula and SI unit.
flashcards.a: Density = Mass / Volume. SI unit: kg m⁻³. It is the mass per unit volume of a substance.

flashcards.q: State the rule for whether an object floats or sinks in a liquid.
flashcards.a: Object floats if its density < fluid density (buoyant force ≥ weight). Object sinks if its density > fluid density (buoyant force < weight).

flashcards.q: Why does a cork float and an iron nail sink in water?
flashcards.a: Density of cork < density of water → buoyant force > weight → floats. Density of iron > density of water → buoyant force < weight → sinks.

flashcards.q: Why does a ship made of iron and steel float, while a flat iron sheet sinks?
flashcards.a: A ship is hollow, so its average density (metal + enclosed air) is less than that of water. A flat iron sheet has no enclosed air, so its density far exceeds that of water — it sinks.

flashcards.q: Name two devices based on Archimedes' Principle.
flashcards.a: Lactometer (to test purity of milk) and Hydrometer (to measure density of liquids).

flashcards.q: Why does a bucket of water feel lighter when drawn from inside a well while still submerged?
flashcards.a: The water exerts a buoyant force on the bucket when submerged, reducing its apparent weight. Once out of the water, there is no buoyant force, so it feels heavier.

flashcards.q: True or False: The gravitational force between two ordinary-sized objects (e.g., two people sitting nearby) is felt in everyday life.
flashcards.a: False. The gravitational force between ordinary objects is extremely small because G is very small. Only large masses (like planets) produce noticeable gravitational effects.

flashcards.q: What is the apparent weight of an object in a fluid, according to Archimedes' principle?
flashcards.a: Apparent weight = Actual weight − Buoyant force = mg − (weight of fluid displaced).

flashcards.q: How does pressure applied at one point of a confined fluid behave?
flashcards.a: It is transmitted undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid.

flashcards.q: What is the weight in newtons of a 10 kg object on the moon? (g on moon ≈ 1.63 m s⁻²)
